summary: In this presentation, Aviv Regev pitches the creation of the Human Cell Atlas. She argues that advances in single-cell genomics make it possible to build a comprehensive reference map of all human cells, which is essential for connecting genotypes to phenotypes. She advocates for a large-scale, standardized consortium approach to avoid technical noise and outlines a sampling strategy that relies on cartographic principles rather than measuring every single cell in the body.

principles.statement: Cells are the critical intermediate between genotype and phenotype.
principles.rationale: To functionally characterize genetic variants and understand how they cause disease, we must know exactly which cells those variants manifest in.
principles.source_quote: Now, cells are also a key intermediate in between the genotype and the phenotypes that we care about so dearly... if we actually want to functionally characterize variants and act upon them we have to know which cells we are dealing with.

principles.statement: Foundational biological catalogs require standardized, large-scale consortium management.
principles.rationale: If data collection is fragmented across different institutes without standardized sampling, prep, and analytics, the resulting catalog will be riddled with technical noise rather than true biological variation.
principles.source_quote: ...only if we do it in a standardized way from sampling acquisition, to sample prep, to QC, to analytics would we ever have a chance of actually creating a meaningful catalog that is not riddled by noise. And our body didn’t hear that it actually belongs to different institutes.

principles.statement: You do not need to measure every single cell to build a comprehensive biological atlas.
principles.rationale: By applying the principles of cartography and sampling, we can measure orders of magnitude fewer cells and still achieve the necessary resolution for an appropriate survey of the human body.
principles.source_quote: And what is very critical is the fact that in order to build an atlas, we don't have to measure every single cell. In the same sense that when we chart the map we actually don’t do it in a one-to-one scale...

frameworks.name: Cellular Atlas Dimensions
frameworks.when_to_apply: When defining the scope and requirements for a comprehensive reference map of biological tissues or organisms.
steps: Distinguish between different states for the same cell type (e.g., naive vs. activated).
Position all cells in their three-dimensional spatial locations.
Identify salient features as cells undergo dynamic transitions (e.g., differentiation).
Trace the entire developmental lineage of the cells.
frameworks.source_quote: Well, obviously the first thing that should be there is the listing of all of the different cell types... We also want to be able to distinguish between different states... position all of these cells in their three dimensional locations... identify the salient features as these cells undergo dynamic transitions... and finally in an ideal world we'll also be able to trace their entire lineage...

mental_models.name: The Sequencer as a Microscope
mental_models.description: Viewing high-throughput single-cell sequencing not merely as a genetic tool, but as a high-resolution lens to observe the types, states, and physical locations of cells, analogous to how a microscope visualizes physical structures.
mental_models.example: Using single-cell RNA-Seq coupled with spatial informatics to map cells back to their 3D positions in a zebrafish embryo.

mental_models.name: Cartographic Scaling
mental_models.description: Applying map-making principles to biology: just as a geographic map uses a scale (e.g., 1:1000) rather than a 1:1 representation of the earth, a biological atlas can use statistical sampling to accurately represent a tissue without measuring every constituent cell.
mental_models.example: Calculating that only 40,000 cells are required to sufficiently survey the 150 million neurons in the highly complex human retina.

heuristics: Start large-scale mapping projects with a pilot in a few complementary systems (e.g., blood, gut, liver) before scaling to the whole organism.
For current single-cell RNA-Seq, you get the best return on investment from the first 100,000 reads per cell; beyond that is mostly diminishing returns.

signature_quotes.text: Now, cells are also a key intermediate in between the genotype and the phenotypes that we care about so dearly.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why a cell atlas is necessary for understanding genetic variants and disease.

signature_quotes.text: So this all suggests that the sequencer may very well become the microscope for the 21st century.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the impact of single-cell genomics and spatial mapping.

signature_quotes.text: ...our body didn’t hear that it actually belongs to different institutes.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing for a unified, standardized consortium approach rather than fragmented, multi-institute efforts that introduce batch effects.

signature_quotes.text: In the same sense that when we chart the map we actually don’t do it in a one-to-one scale, we do in it a one-to-one hundred, or one-to-one thousand, the level of resolution that is needed.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why we don't need to sequence all 20 trillion human cells to build the Human Cell Atlas.

anti_patterns.description: Building foundational biological datasets through fragmented, unstandardized efforts across different labs.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It introduces massive technical noise and batch effects, making it impossible to distinguish true biological variation from differences in sample prep or analytics.

anti_patterns.description: Over-sequencing single cells (e.g., >500,000 reads per cell) with current molecular biology protocols.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Due to the conversion rate limits of current protocols, sequencing too deeply mostly yields duplicate reads, wasting money for diminishing returns.
